title: Graviton-mediated entanglement due to light bending from a quantum rotor
authors: Dripto Biswas, Sougato Bose, Anupam Mazumdar, Marko Toroš
abstract: One of the key tests of the quantum nature of gravity is to test whether the virtual mediator of gravity between matter and photon gives rise to the quantum light-bending phenomenon. The off-shell degrees of freedom, involving the spin-2 and spin-0 components of graviton, reproduce the classical deviation of light rays, as well as have been predicted to generate entanglement between matter and photon. This paper explores the generation of entanglement due to the quantum gravitational interaction in an optomechanical setup with a quantum rotor and photon. The virtual exchange of a graviton provides entanglement between the photon degrees of freedom and the spatial position of the quantum rotor, with the rotational state affecting its magnitude. We analyze the case of a high spinning rotor, in an approximately classical state of angular momentum, and quantify its effect on the gravitationally induced entanglement between the photon and the position of the quantum rotor. We show that the difference in the linear entanglement entropies, of prograde-and-retrograde motion of the photon with respect to the quantum rotor, provide tangible observable consequences.
summary: This theoretical paper explores how the quantum nature of gravity can be tested by measuring entanglement between a photon and a spinning mechanical rotor. The interaction is mediated by virtual gravitons, leading to corrections in the system's Hamiltonian proportional to the rotor's angular momentum $J$. The key prediction is that the difference in entanglement entropy for clockwise versus counter-clockwise light propagation directly measures this spin-dependent gravitational coupling.
main_problem: The paper investigates testing the quantum nature of gravity by calculating graviton-mediated entanglement between a photon and the spatial position of a quantum rotor.
main_result: The difference in linear entanglement entropies for prograde versus retrograde photon motion provides a measurable signature proportional to the rotor's angular momentum, $J$.
method: The analysis uses quantum field theory techniques applied to an optomechanical setup, deriving effective Hamiltonians and calculating entanglement entropy using Gaussian approximations.
model_or_system: The system is an optomechanical setup involving a photon mode (optical cavity) coupled to a mechanical oscillator (quantum rotor) via the exchange of virtual gravitons. The interaction strength depends on the rotor's angular momentum $J$.
key_observables: Gravitationally induced entanglement entropy, specifically the difference in linear entanglement entropies ($\Delta S$) for prograde and retrograde motion.
important_parameters: Angular momentum ($J$), coupling constants ($g_0$), mechanical frequency ($\omega_m$), and cavity field amplitude ($\alpha$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the semi-classical limit for $J$ (or treats it as a classical background) and focuses on leading order corrections linear in $J$. It also ignores decoherence effects.
figures_summary: Not specified by notes, but calculations involve deriving potentials ($V(d)$), coupling strengths ($g(J)_{0,\sigma}$), and entanglement entropy evolution over time ($	ext{Tr} ho_b^2(t)$).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework using off-shell graviton amplitudes, deriving effective potentials incorporating spin corrections, constructing a full Hamiltonian for the coupled system, and finally calculating the entanglement entropy difference as the key observable.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum information theory (entanglement measures) with fundamental physics (quantum gravity effects), providing concrete, measurable predictions based on rotational dynamics in an optomechanical setting.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the setup showing the various quantities defined in Sec. III. At the centre there is a mass in a ground state initially |0⟩, rotating counterclockwise with an angular momentum J. The opti- cal beam (purple) lies in the XY plane, and the tangential direction senses (σ = ±1), e.g, the photon beam can prograde and retrograde with respect to the rotation of the mass at the centre. We assume that the photon is in a coherent state |α⟩.
